title: Pivot 1 — Where Did Submit Go? (GET vs POST)
description: How a submitted form actually travels through CodeIgniter 3: controllers, GET vs POST, what CI3's 404 proves, and the line between client and server.
congratsText: You mastered Pivot 1 — GET vs POST!

sections.title: Objectives
sections.lesson:
Objectives
  • Explain what happens the moment you click Submit
  • Tell GET and POST apart by where the data goes
  • Explain what CI3's 404 error actually proves

sections.title: Click Submit — Where Does It Go?
sections.lesson:
Click Submit — Where Does It Go?
You fill out a form and click Submit. The browser packs up what you typed and sends it somewhere.
That "somewhere" is set by the form's action attribute. Point it at a destination that isn't ready to catch it, and the trip goes nowhere.
<form action="pivot/login" method="post">
A plain HTML page cannot read what you typed. Something else has to catch the data.

quiz.question: In the form below, which attribute decides where the submitted data travels?
options: action
method
name
id

sections.title: Client vs Server — Two Computers, Two Jobs
sections.lesson:
Client vs Server — Two Computers, Two Jobs
Two computers are always involved. The client is the browser on your screen. The server is the computer that stores and runs the site's files.
Think of a restaurant. You, the client, place an order. The kitchen, the server, is the only place that can cook it and send it back.
The browser can show a page. It cannot run PHP, save data, or check a password. Only the server can do that.

quiz.question: In the restaurant idea from this lesson, what does the server represent?
options: The kitchen that prepares the order
The customer placing the order
The menu sitting on the table
The receipt at the end of the meal

sections.title: A Framework Needs a Controller
sections.lesson:
A Framework Needs a Controller
In plain PHP, any .php file can run on its own. CI3 works differently — every request has to land on a controller method.
A controller is a PHP class that extends CI_Controller. Each public method inside it is something a URL can call.
class Pivot extends CI_Controller { public function hello() { echo "Hello from CI3"; } }
Save that as Pivot.php inside application/controllers/, and CI3 can now run it.

quiz.question: What must a PHP class do before CI3 will treat it as a controller?
options: Extend CI_Controller
Save with a .html extension
Sit outside the application folder
Start every method name with get_

sections.title: Segments in the URL — Controller and Method
sections.lesson:
Segments in the URL — Controller and Method
Visit index.php/pivot/hello, and CI3 reads the address in two pieces, called segments.
The first segment, pivot, names the controller class. The second segment, hello, names the method inside it.
index.php/pivot/hello controller method
Change either segment, and CI3 looks for a different class or a different method, not the same page under a new name.

quiz.question: In the URL index.php/pivot/hello, what does the segment hello tell CI3 to run?
options: The method inside the Pivot controller
The name of the project folder
The database table to query
The file extension to load

sections.title: The GET Method — Data Rides in the URL
sections.lesson:
The GET Method — Data Rides in the URL
Set a form's method to get, and the browser tacks every field onto the end of the web address.
<form action="index.php/pivot/login" method="get">
Submit a login form with GET, and the address bar shows something like this:
index.php/pivot/login?username=criscel&password=1234
Anyone who looks at your screen, or your browser history, can read that value.

quiz.question: A form uses method="get" and is submitted. Where does the typed data end up?
options: Attached to the end of the URL
Hidden inside the request body
Saved straight to the database
Stored only in browser memory

sections.title: Seeing GET's Danger — A Password in Plain Sight
sections.lesson:
Seeing GET's Danger — A Password in Plain Sight
The login form from an earlier lab asks for a username and a password. Switch its method to GET and submit it.
The password now sits in the address bar for anyone nearby to read. It also gets saved in the browser's history.
This is why GET is never used for passwords, or for any data that should stay private — framework or no framework.

quiz.question: A student submits the login form with method="get" while a classmate is watching the screen. What can the classmate see?
options: The typed password, sitting in the address bar
Nothing, GET data is always hidden
Only the username, never the password
A 404 error instead of the login page

sections.title: The POST Method — Data Hides in the Request
sections.lesson:
The POST Method — Data Hides in the Request
Set method to post instead, and the same fields travel inside the request body, not the URL.
<form action="index.php/pivot/login" method="post">
Submit that form and the address bar stays clean: just index.php/pivot/login, no visible password.
GET
Data shows in the URL. Fine for a search box.
POST
Data hides in the body. Use for passwords and anything private.

quiz.question: Which situation is method="post" meant for?
options: Submitting a login form with a password
Running a search box for public results
Sharing a link to a specific page
Bookmarking a page in the browser

sections.title: CI3's 404 — Proof the Controller Wasn't There
sections.lesson:
CI3's 404 — Proof the Controller Wasn't There
Point a form's action at a method that doesn't exist yet, like pivot/store, and submit it.
CI3 shows its own 404 error page. That error is good news: it proves CI3 received the request and searched for a matching controller and method.
A 404 means "I looked, and found nothing to run." It's proof the trip worked, the destination just isn't built yet.

quiz.question: A form submits to pivot/store, which does not exist yet. CI3 shows a 404 error. What does that error actually prove?
options: CI3 received the request and found no matching controller or method
The form's HTML contains a typo
The internet connection was interrupted
The browser blocked the form from submitting

sections.title: Reading Segments Wrong — Controller vs Method Typos
sections.lesson:
Reading Segments Wrong — Controller vs Method Typos
Misspell the method segment, like pivot/helo, and CI3 still finds the Pivot controller — it just has no matching method inside it.
Misspell the controller segment instead, like pivott/store, and CI3 never finds a matching class at all.
Both mistakes end in the same 404 page, but for a different reason — one is a missing method, the other is a missing class.

quiz.question: A URL reads index.php/pivott/store — the controller name is misspelled. What is CI3 unable to find?
options: A controller class matching that name
A method matching that name
A database table matching that name
A view file matching that name

sections.title: Catching the Request for Real
sections.lesson:
Catching the Request for Real
Build store() inside Pivot.php for real this time, with one line of code:
public function store() { print_r($this->input->post()); }
Submit the form now, and the page prints every field you typed, labeled by its name attribute.
$this->input->post() is CI3's own way of reading $_POST. No name attribute, no entry in the array.

quiz.question: A form has <input name="item">. It submits to store(), which contains print_r($this->input->post());. What appears on screen?
options: Array ( [item] => the typed value )
Array ( [0] => the typed value )
The literal text $this->input->post()
A blank page with no output

sections.title: Recap
sections.lesson:
Recap
  • Submit sends your data to whatever controller and method the form's action points to
  • The client is the browser; only the server can run PHP and save data
  • CI3 only runs classes that extend CI_Controller, saved inside application/controllers/
  • A URL's segments name the controller first, then the method
  • GET attaches your data to the URL, in plain view
  • Never use GET for a password, it shows on screen and saves in history
  • POST hides your data inside the request body, not the URL
  • CI3's 404 proves it searched for a controller and method, and found neither
  • A misspelled controller and a misspelled method both 404, for different reasons
  • CI3 reads POST data through $this->input->post(), keyed by each field's name attribute
